---
title: "Patents, Papers, Parts & Planet"
subtitle: "Mapping Local Innovation Trajectories in Synthetic Biology"
author: "CSH Thesis · Supervised by Frank Neffke & Claudia Doblinger"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    center: false
    width: 1200
    height: 700
    margin: 0.08
    highlight-style: github
    footer: "Patents, Papers, Parts & Planet — CSH Thesis"
---

## The question

**Do iGEM student projects and academic papers in synthetic biology cluster together semantically — in the same cities?**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Synthetic biology is a rapidly growing field
- Innovation is not evenly distributed geographically
- Cities tend to specialize in related knowledge domains
- **iGEM** introduces a novel artifact: open-science student competition projects
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Three artifact types:**

🧬 iGEM projects (4,615)

📄 Academic papers (8,720)

📋 ~~Patents~~ *(not available)*
:::
:::

::: {.callout-note}
We test **semantic relatedness**, not causation. Both artifact types may reflect the same underlying city-level specialisation.
:::

---

## Why iGEM?

iGEM (International Genetically Engineered Machine) is a global student competition founded at MIT in 2004.

- Teams design biological systems using open-source "BioBrick" parts
- All parts deposited in a public registry (~90,000 parts total)
- Projects documented on public wikis
- Hosted at universities → embedded in local academic culture

> "Do cities that produce focused iGEM activity also produce focused academic output in the same synthetic biology subfield?"

---

## Data

| Source | Records | Years | Cities |
|--------|--------:|------:|-------:|
| OpenAlex (papers) | 8,720 | 2000–2026 | 1,146 |
| iGEM Registry (projects) | 4,615 | 2009–2025 | 626 |
| **Both types** | **13,335** | | **379 shared** |

**Carbon capture case study:**

- 234 papers + 141 projects flagged by keyword match
- Keywords: *carbon capture, CO₂ fixation, RuBisCO, biofuel, Calvin cycle,* …

---

## Method: SPECTER2 embeddings

Each artifact → 768-dimensional vector using **SPECTER2** (AllenAI, 2022)

- Trained on scientific papers with citation supervision
- *Proximity adapter* optimised for document-to-document similarity
- More domain-appropriate than BERT or GPT for scientific text

Then: **UMAP** to 30D (clustering) and 2D (visualization)

Then: **HDBSCAN** → 347 clusters; top 25 labeled with Claude Haiku

---

## The semantic space

*Papers (blue) and iGEM projects (orange) interleave across semantic space*

*Carbon-capture artifacts (green ◆) span several adjacent subfield clusters*

→ See [interactive figure](results.qmd) on the Results page

---

## Thematic clusters (top 10 by size)

| Cluster | Size |
|---------|-----:|
| Plastic degradation and biodegradation | 191 |
| Antimicrobial peptides for crop protection | 189 |
| Bacteriophage therapy and engineering | 115 |
| Microbial chassis and metabolic engineering | 114 |
| Engineered bacteria for cancer therapy | 110 |
| CRISPR-Cas genome editing systems | 90 |
| Biofuel and fine chemical production | 79 |
| Minimal genome design and essentiality | 75 |
| DNA assembly automation standards | 73 |
| Biosensor / point-of-care detection | 55 |

---

## City-level semantic overlap

For each city with papers **and** projects:

$$\text{overlap}_i = \cos\left(\bar{e}^{\text{papers}}_i,\; \bar{e}^{\text{projects}}_i\right)$$

**379 cities** · Range: 0.80 – 0.99 · Mean: 0.94

Top cities: Cambridge, Shanghai, Tianjin, Berkeley, Boston

→ High-overlap cities are large, focused synthetic biology centres

---

## Regression results

| Variable | (1) | (2) | (3) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| log(papers) | 0.017\*\*\* | 0.017\*\*\* | 0.017\*\*\* |
| log(projects) | 0.019\*\*\* | 0.010\*\*\* | 0.010\*\*\* |
| iGEM year span | — | 0.002\*\*\* | 0.002\*\*\* |
| CC paper share | — | — | 0.019 |
| CC project share | — | — | −0.009 |
| **R²** | **0.62** | **0.63** | **0.64** |

*\*\*\* p < 0.01, HC3 robust SE, N = 379*

**Key finding:** More papers + more projects + longer iGEM engagement → higher semantic alignment

---

## Case study: Carbon capture

7 cities with ≥2 CC papers and ≥2 CC projects:

| City | CC Papers | CC Projects | CC Overlap |
|------|----------:|------------:|-----------:|
| San Diego | 3 | 3 | 0.963 |
| Wuhan | 2 | 5 | 0.959 |
| Beijing | 10 | 2 | 0.956 |
| Shanghai | 2 | 6 | 0.948 |
| Madison | 3 | 2 | 0.929 |
| Austin | 2 | 2 | 0.923 |

→ Small sample, but expected hubs. Consistently high overlap (0.92–0.96).

---

## Limitations

1. **No patents** — the downstream translation step cannot be tested
2. **SPECTER2 domain mismatch** — trained on papers, not project descriptions
3. **Cross-sectional** — cannot determine temporal ordering within cities
4. **Single-institution geocoding** — multi-city collaborations not captured
5. **Carbon-capture sample is small** — 7 cities limits statistical power

---

## Conclusion

**Cities with larger, more sustained synthetic biology footprints show higher semantic alignment between their iGEM projects and academic papers.**

- R² ≈ 0.62 from size alone; temporal engagement adds further explanatory power
- Carbon capture case study identifies the expected geographic hubs
- Results are *descriptive*: both artifact types likely reflect the same underlying city-level specialisation

**Next steps:**
- Add patent data (PatentsView, better geocoding)
- Longitudinal analysis: does project activity in year $t$ predict paper activity in $t+k$?
- Extend to other synthetic biology subfields beyond carbon capture

---

## Thank you

**Full website:** [zer0juice.github.io/synbiomap](https://zer0juice.github.io/synbiomap)

**Code:** [github.com/Zer0Juice/synbiomap](https://github.com/Zer0Juice/synbiomap)

**Supervisors:** Frank Neffke (CSH) · Claudia Doblinger (TU Munich)
